slides.headline: The Conditional Activation Model
slides.subhead: Uncovering Policy Demand Responses to AI-Driven Automation
slides.notes: Open with the aggregate null puzzle: why do information experiments on automation risk so often produce null or conflicting effects? The CAM framework resolves this as a composition effect.

blocks.label: Author
blocks.text: Ignacio Urbina · Stony Brook University · April 2026

slides.headline: AI automation is reshaping labor markets — and voters know it.
slides.subhead: 47% of U.S. occupations face high automation exposure (Frey & Osborne 2017). Pew (2023): 62% of Americans believe AI will have a major impact on workers. Yet evidence that risk information moves policy preferences is surprisingly weak.
slides.notes: Set the stakes: this is not an abstract information-processing question. Governments are making real policy decisions about retraining, regulation, and safety nets. Whether citizens respond to risk information determines whether democratic demand signals reach policymakers.

items.title: Labor market transformation
items.detail: Routine cognitive and manual tasks increasingly automatable

items.title: High public salience
items.detail: Majorities report concern about AI's workforce impact

items.title: Policy stakes
items.detail: Retraining programs, AI regulation, UBI, worker protections

items.title: The gap
items.detail: If risk info does not move preferences, demand signals never reach policymakers

slides.headline: Standard model: personal risk information should increase policy demand.
slides.subhead: Self-interest theory (Meltzer & Richard 1981), motivated reasoning under threat (Albertson & Gadarian 2015), and egotropic voting (Kinder & Kiewiet 1981) all predict that learning your job is at risk should increase demand for government protection.
slides.notes: Three theoretical traditions converge on the same prediction. Self-interest: threatened workers demand redistribution. Threat motivation: anxiety focuses attention on protective policies. Egotropic economics: personal economic shocks drive policy preferences. The prediction is clear and well-grounded. So why doesn't it consistently hold?

items.label: Self-interest
items.detail: Threatened workers seek redistribution (Meltzer & Richard 1981)

items.label: Threat motivation
items.detail: Threat focuses attention on protection (Albertson & Gadarian 2015)

items.label: Egotropic voting
items.detail: Economic shocks drive preferences (Kinder & Kiewiet 1981)

slides.headline: But experimental results are mixed — often null.
slides.subhead: A growing body of experiments provides information about personal automation risk and measures downstream policy preferences. The results do not converge.
slides.notes: Walk through each study. The audience should feel the accumulating tension: a reasonable prediction, repeatedly failing to replicate. The key pivot comes from Arntz et al., who show that OPPOSING subgroup effects can cancel in the aggregate.

items.title: Zhang & Hou (2022)
items.detail: Information about automation risk → null effect on policy preferences

items.title: Magistro et al. (2024)
items.detail: Automation risk information → null aggregate effect on redistribution

items.title: Gallego et al. (2022)
items.detail: Strong positive effects — but different treatment and sample

items.title: Arntz et al. (2024)
items.detail: Opposing subgroup effects that cancel in the aggregate

items.title: Wu (2022), Im (2023)
items.detail: Conditional effects that depend on ideology, education, or prior beliefs

slides.headline: The problem is not the prediction — it's the estimand.
slides.subhead: Unconditional ATEs average over heterogeneous subgroups. When subgroups respond in opposing directions (or not at all), the aggregate washes out. The question is not whether effects exist — but for whom and under what conditions.
slides.notes: This is the pivotal slide. The reframe: null ATEs are not evidence of preference stability. They are evidence of heterogeneity that cancels. Arntz et al. showed this empirically; the CAM provides the theoretical architecture for predicting who responds and who does not.

left.label: Wrong question
left.text: Does automation risk information increase policy demand?

right.label: Right question
right.text: For whom, and under what conditions, does automation risk information activate policy demand?

slides.headline: Two sequential gates between information and policy demand.
slides.subhead: Gate 1 (source trust) filters who processes the information as credible. Gate 2 (belief gap × trait anxiety) determines who translates processed information into policy search.
slides.notes: Gate 1 is discrete (accept/reject), not continuous — this is a departure from standard persuasion models. Gate 2 requires BOTH a positive informational shock AND dispositional vigilance. Below either threshold, even credible information produces cool cognition: belief updating without policy urgency.

items.title: Gate 1 is discrete
items.detail: Trust/reject, not a continuous moderator — departure from standard persuasion models

items.title: Gate 2 requires joint activation
items.detail: Neither belief gap alone nor anxiety alone is sufficient

items.title: Cool cognition below threshold
items.detail: Belief updating without policy urgency — information is processed but does not motivate action

slides.headline: Three cognitive pathways explain the mixed literature.
slides.notes: Source rejection: ~62% of respondents. Shallow priming from treatment framing, but zero modulation by belief gap or anxiety. Cool cognition: trusters with low anxiety update beliefs without urgency. Deep persuasion: trusters with high anxiety AND large belief gaps — both gates satisfied.

items.label: 1. Source rejection
items.detail: Skeptics: information discounted at intake; shallow priming only

items.label: 2. Cool cognition
items.detail: Belief updating without urgency; no policy search triggered

items.label: 3. Deep persuasion
items.detail: Both gates satisfied; sustained elaboration → policy demand

blocks.label: Implication
blocks.text: Null aggregate ATEs are a composition artifact — heterogeneous subgroups that cancel, not genuine preference stability.

slides.headline: Pre-registered experiment, N = 901 U.S. workers.
slides.subhead: Between-subjects (475 treated, 426 control), block randomized by occupation. Three-step belief correction protocol — not a standard vignette.
slides.notes: The treatment creates individualized treatment intensity. Workers compress beliefs toward 30-40 regardless of actual risk (regression to the mean). Metacognitive failure: high certainty in priors does NOT predict smaller errors (r = -0.004). Mean gap = 2.44 but SD = 36.

items: Elicit subjective risk belief (0–100 percentile scale)
Reveal expert estimate; visualize the belief gap

left.label: Key
left.text: Individualized treatment intensity — the belief gap varies naturally across respondents.

items.title: Outcome
items.detail: Policy demand index: 5 items on 7-pt scale (ω = 0.85)

items.title: Gate 1
items.detail: Source trust: 5-pt item, post-treatment

items.title: Gate 2a
items.detail: Belief gap: expert percentile − subjective prior

items.title: Gate 2b
items.detail: Trait anxiety: 4 items, pre-treatment (ω = 0.90)

slides.headline: Trust is a post-treatment collider — naive approaches fail.
slides.subhead: Observed trust is caused by both dispositional trust (S) and the belief gap. The BFMM blocks this via principal stratification with Gap and Gap² as covariates.
slides.notes: The core identification problem: trust is measured only for treated units and is caused by both the latent disposition AND the treatment (via belief gap). Solution: include Gap and Gap-squared in the ordinal logistic model to block the backdoor path and isolate the dispositional component.

items.title: Collider bias
items.detail: Gap → Q_obs ← S; conditioning conflates congruence with disposition

items.title: Post-treatment
items.detail: Trust observed only for treated; breaks randomization

items.title: Measurement error
items.detail: Latent constructs with non-trivial error; attenuation up to 50%

slides.headline: Unconditional ATE: positive, modest, robust across estimators.
slides.subhead: All four estimators converge — but the aggregate understates the responsive subgroup by a factor of 2.5.
slides.notes: This is the starting point. The BFMM reproduces the unconditional ATE exactly while also decomposing it. The modest effect is real — the question is where it concentrates.

slides.headline: Trust is a discrete gate — Skeptics show null effects everywhere.
slides.notes: This is the core Gate 1 finding. Even among Skeptics who receive large belief gaps AND are dispositionally high in anxiety, treatment effects remain null. Trust is NOT a continuous moderator — it's a binary filter.

left.label: Skeptics (≈62%)
left.detail: Gap β = −0.06 · Anxiety β = −0.06 · Interaction β = −0.07 — all null

right.label: Trusters (≈38%)
right.detail: Gap β = 0.14 · Anxiety β = 0.14 · Interaction β = 0.13 — all positive

blocks.label: Implication
blocks.text: Source rejection is strict: zero modulation by informational shock or dispositional vigilance. This contrasts with standard persuasion models where trust is continuous.

slides.headline: Among Trusters: joint activation drives the headline effect.
slides.subhead: Neither belief gap alone nor anxiety alone is sufficient. The interaction is the mechanism.
slides.notes: Activation threshold at ~20th percentile of trait anxiety. Below this, even Trusters with large gaps show null effects (cool cognition). Above threshold, effects scale linearly with both Gap and Anxiety. All pairwise contrasts P(delta > 0) > 0.97.

items.label: Baseline CATE
items.detail: mean Gap × mean Anxiety among Trusters

items.label: Maximum CATE
items.detail: high Gap × high Anxiety — 2.5× the ATE

items.title: Activation threshold
items.detail: 20th percentile of trait anxiety (z ≈ −0.84)

items.title: Below threshold
items.detail: Even large gaps → null effects (cool cognition)

items.title: Above threshold
items.detail: Effects scale linearly with Gap × Anxiety

items.title: All pairwise contrasts
items.detail: P(Δ > 0) > 0.97 for high–high vs. all other cells

slides.headline: Null aggregate effects are a composition artifact, not preference stability.
slides.subhead: The two-gate framework transfers to any persuasion domain with expert sources and dispositional moderators.
slides.notes: Scope conditions: U.S. workers, liberal skew (62% Democrat), single trust measure, immediate outcomes, Frey & Osborne 2017 risk estimates. Future work: manipulate source credibility orthogonally, test in climate/vaccine/trade domains, measure persistence.

items: Two-gate model resolves conflicting findings as composition effects.
BFMM handles latent classes with collider-biased post-treatment moderators.
Maximum CATE (d = 0.61) more than doubles the unconditional ATE.
Identifies responsive subgroup for targeted risk communication.

right.label: Scope
right.text: N = 901 U.S. workers (CloudResearch). 62% Democrat. Immediate post-treatment. Frey & Osborne 2017 estimates.

right.label: Transfers to
right.text: Climate, vaccines, trade — any domain where expert information meets dispositional heterogeneity.
